chapters.title: Luck hides more than people think
chapters.text:
The stock market shows this pattern in a clear way. Some investors get labeled as gifted simply because they have been on a winning streak. Their good results get chalked up to skill when chance played a much larger part.

Some fields do require real skill to succeed. A long career in medicine or law does not fall into your lap by luck. Training and ability matter there.

The stock market is different. Real rules are hard to pin down, and chance drives a lot of the outcome. Because of the built-in randomness of markets, an investor with no skill can post the same returns as a skilled one over many years.

In fact, 10,000 investors who pick stocks at random have about a 45 percent chance of finishing a year in the green. That number hints at a strange truth. You could do just as well by flipping a coin.

From that crowd of 10,000 random investors, close to 200 will log a profit every year for five years running. People will start to call them talented, but in truth they just guessed and got lucky.

Over the long run, the truth comes out. Many of those lucky investors will one day lose everything in a single bad quarter, wiping out years of gains.

The rest of this summary looks at how we mistake randomness for cause and effect, why we keep underestimating chance, and what we can do about it.

chapters.title: Change is the one constant
chapters.text:
Empirical science builds its ideas through a process called induction. It depends on what people observe. Picture someone who sees hundreds of goats and notices they are all black. They might conclude that every goat is black.

The moment a goat of a different color shows up, that conclusion falls apart. The problem of induction says that no theory can ever be proven right. It can only be proven wrong. So theories keep shifting as new evidence rolls in.

This view applies to investing as well. Any theory you trust can break at any time. Build that possibility into the shape of your portfolio. Using the past to predict the future is a recipe for disaster.

Human choice keeps changing the world, and adaptation is constant. As people adjust to new realities, business changes too. The patterns that seemed solid in the past will shift, which removes the very effect you expected to see.

Most people pick the familiar over the new. Evolution shows that even unfit creatures sometimes beat the odds. Survival of the fittest only describes the average outcome, not the only possible one.

The QWERTY keyboard is a good example. It was built to stop keys from jamming on old typewriters. Once typing technology improved, no one wanted to switch. A better layout could have won, but QWERTY had already become the norm.

This is called a path dependent outcome. Some products stay on top simply because they crossed a tipping point. Microsoft got big because people bought what their friends and coworkers were using. The choice had little to do with quality.

Tipping points are nonlinear, which makes them hard to predict. A tiny input can spark a huge shift. The truth is that people often give up just before they would have broken through. The extra mile is uneven for everyone. Some hit it fast, others take much longer, and there is no standard rule to follow. Chance plays a role here too.

No matter how sharp our choices or how well we play the odds, randomness always has the last word, as Nassim Nicholas Taleb has observed.

chapters.title: Context shapes how we see outcomes
chapters.text:
We live in an age of nonstop information. At the same time, our minds are not built to weigh the probabilities that come with all that data. To keep up, we fall back on rules that oversimplify things and on assumptions that cloud our judgment.

The result is a steady stream of biases. Psychologists have shown that we carry many kinds of biases, and they keep us from seeing situations clearly. Attribution bias, for example, is why we credit hard work for our wins and blame bad luck for our losses.

The path that leads to an outcome shapes how we feel about it. This is called path dependence. Losses sting more than wins of the same size. Imagine winning $50,000 today and losing $40,000 tomorrow. That result feels worse than simply winning $10,000 tomorrow with no losses along the way. The end number is the same, but the path makes the feeling different.

When the first idea in a chain of thoughts sets the tone, those beliefs are path dependent. Path dependence also keeps us tied to old opinions even when fresh evidence pushes back. We repeat old arguments and dig in rather than update our views.

Parents can fall into the same trap. They have invested years in their children and find it hard to step back. That habit can backfire. The healthy move is to contradict yourself on purpose and update your views. Remind yourself that your children are adults now and can make their own calls.

A mistake is not something to judge after the fact, but in light of the information available until that point, as Nassim Nicholas Taleb has written.

chapters.title: Emotions can lift or sink our choices
chapters.text:
Decision making gets easier when emotions are in the mix. When two options look equally good, pure logic leaves you stuck. An emotional nudge, even a small one, is often what tips the scale.

Neurobiologists have found that feelings come before logic. Our rational mind is really just looking for reasons to justify the feelings we already have. Reason is not the strongest force in the room. Being aware of this is important when a clear-headed choice is needed. An investor, for example, has to learn to set emotions aside so that impulse does not lead to losses.

Past experience is of little use when the future is up for grabs. Investors keep fooling themselves into thinking the market will not crash, or that they will see a crash coming. Psychologists call this hindsight bias. We look at past events and build a mental model to predict when they will happen again. The problem is that the model only works for those exact past conditions. If a new situation looks nothing like the past, the model fails, and we are fooled by randomness.

In the markets, traders use backtesters to build trading rules. They comb through past data and design rules that would have worked back then. Modern computers make this kind of analysis powerful. But the past results came from chance as much as skill. Those rules may not work in the next market cycle.

chapters.title: Caught off guard by the unexpected
chapters.text:
The French writer Henry de Montherlant, a nobleman, chose to end his life after learning that a degenerative condition was robbing him of his sight. His choice clashes with the Stoic view, which pushes for something different. Stoics teach that we should take charge of our fate, especially when outcomes are uncertain. We always have a choice, to give up or to accept what life puts in front of us. In a world of randomness, our power shows up in how we respond to small events and big ones alike.

Unexpected events are constant. Hedge funds often blame their losses on surprises their models never planned for. Their models fail because they leave no room for chance. A single random event can wreck an expected outcome. These rare events get ignored in most data analysis because people assume they will not happen.

The COVID-19 pandemic is a clear example of an unexpected event that shook the global economy. Investors are easy prey for such surprises. A model that delivered small wins in the past gets scaled up, leaving the investor wide open to a sudden crash.

It is healthy to be open to serendipity, as long as it does no harm. Outside of investing, chance can be a positive force. Art and poetry, for example, can turn ordinary words into something beautiful. Science and finance, on the other hand, need precision and have little patience for flowery language. So we are fooled by randomness in the first case and need cold logic in the second.

Even when we follow every rule, we can still be hit by harmful randomness. A person who eats well, exercises, and gets enough sleep can still get cancer, while a chain smoker might live a long life and pass quietly in their sleep.

Stoicism is the right response to harmful randomness. We have to be brave and face reality without fear. Self-pity, blame, and self-destruction do not help. The better path is a quiet kind of dignity. Show wisdom and courage when misfortune arrives.

A point worth remembering. Our response to randomness is the only real control we have over random events in our lives.

chapters.title: The media adds a lot of noise
chapters.text:
Checking the news and stock reports every day pays off very little. Social media is full of false and useless stories.

Sorting through useless news to find useful news costs more time than it gives back. Stock prices move around for no clear reason. Trying to explain every wiggle is a waste, since the movement often breaks the rules we think prices should follow. Prices only make sense over long stretches. Short term moves are mostly random noise.

An investor who checks the portfolio often feels more pleasure and more pain than one who checks once a year. Someone who checks every minute may enjoy about 60,688 minutes of pleasure in a year and about 60,271 minutes of pain. Someone who checks once a year for 20 years can expect 19 out of 20 years to feel good.

Even when the end result is the same, the investor who checks all the time ends up emotionally drained, since losses sting more than wins satisfy.

chapters.title: Final thoughts on randomness
chapters.text:
Randomness fools everyone, yet we tend to call it fate. A lucky streak gets turned into a story about skill. The world keeps changing, and those changes overturn yesterday's theories. The best does not always win, and even careful living cannot prevent disaster.

We fail to grasp the weight of randomness because of biases that have built up over time. Our thinking is often tied to a single context and relies on rough rules of thumb. Emotions can cloud our choices, but they can also point us in the right direction. The habit of looking for patterns and explanations in past events makes our predictions of the future weak. The past rarely repeats itself, and we are bad at judging rare events.

To live with randomness, we should enjoy the harmless kind and face the harmful kind with courage, dignity, and responsibility. Resist the pull of random noise from media and the markets.

A small exercise to try. Over the next two weeks, make a point of making decisions that are not driven by emotion. Then check the results and see how it goes.
